text:
This paper attempts to investigate the doctrine and praxis of gratitude as a believer’s response to the receipt of God’s grace. This study primarily resorts to biblical exegesis of 1 Corinthians 1:4-9. The city of Corinth, the historical background of 1 Corinthians, was described as a highly affluent society with great moral decay. Paul’s use of thanksgiving in 1 Corinthians 1:4-9 serves as both a therapeutic and evaluative tool for the church in Corinth. The Corinthian church was a church plagued by strife, divisions and immorality. However Paul begins his treatise in 1 Corinthians by declaring the foundational fact of God’s grace.

In this study the researcher took a descriptive qualitative approach to the Bible by seeking to understand the cause of Paul's thanksgiving in Scripture and its relevance to contemporary Christians. In line with the findings of previous researchers on the Bible, it was found that Paul's understanding of thanksgiving was based on the concept that God is the one initiating salvation, and that human beings do not achieve it through their own works. This study, however, contributed to the body of knowledge by explaining the relevance of Paul's model of thanksgiving in Scripture in the contemporary world. The findings of the study were that the Corinthian Christians in Scripture had been made rich in speech and knowledge, endowed with all gifts and blessed in God's gracious provision to them. It was concluded that Paul's model of thanksgiving in Scripture remains an essential way of reminding contemporary Christians of the need to focus on the manifold blessings of God rather than their achievements, or lack thereof, or even the difficulties they may be passing through. This knowledge, according to Scripture, provides the motivations that lead to the thanksgiving of Christians for God's on-going work of sanctification which assures the believer that God will keep him blameless on the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
